Sh0Sh0 does the boring part — shrink a link — and then hands you the part everyone else hides behind a paywall: analytics, custom codes, password protection, and page hosting. Free, no trial clock.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sh0Sh0’s feature set — analytics, custom codes, password protection, page hosting — all free.There is a specific look people give you when you tell them you built a URL shortener. It is the same look you’d get for announcing you’ve invented a new kind of spoon. The market is, by any reasonable measure, solved. There are dozens of them. Most are free. What exactly, the look asks, did you think you were adding?
Fair. So let me be honest about what bothered me enough to build https://sh0sh0.netlify.app anyway.
The part that’s actually free isn’t the part you need
Here’s the quiet con of the shortener world. Shrinking a link is trivial — it costs essentially nothing, so everyone gives it away. The thing you actually came for is the answer to “did anyone click it, and from where?” And that, mysteriously, is where the pricing table appears. You paste a link, you get a short one, and the moment you ask a follow-up question, a tier called “Pro” materializes out of the fog.
So the free product is a redirect. The useful product costs a monthly fee. I found that arrangement irritating enough to invert it.
What Sh0Sh0 actually does
The surface is exactly what you’d expect, and I won’t insult you by dressing it up: you paste a long, unspeakable URL — the kind with forty characters of tracking junk welded to the end — and you get back something short and shareable. Every shortener on earth does this. The difference is everything downstream of that link.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The landing page: drop in a monstrous URL, get a clean link back.
• Real-time analytics — clicks, locations, devices, as they happen. Not a blurred preview chart with “upgrade to see more” stamped across it. The actual numbers.

• Custom short codes — so your link can say something instead of looking like a license plate.

• Password-protected links — for the times a link genuinely shouldn’t be open to anyone who stumbles onto it.

• HTML page hosting — so a short link can serve a real little page, not just bounce you somewhere else.
This is, almost exactly, the feature set that competing “simple, free” shorteners describe as premium, enterprise, or regrettably-not-on-your-plan. Sh0Sh0 treats it as the default. Free forever, and — a detail I’m weirdly proud of — with no free-trial countdown ticking in the corner reminding you that your relationship with the tool has a billing date.
A short walk-through
The landing page does the one job a landing page should: there’s a field, you drop your monstrous URL in, you get a clean link back. The whole thing is over before a heavier app would have finished loading its cookie banner.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The trending-links view — a quick glance at what’s moving.There’s a trending-links view — a quick glance at what’s moving — and a features section that lays out the analytics, custom codes, hosting, and password protection in plain sight rather than behind a “contact sales” button. The how-it-works section is mercifully short, mostly because the product genuinely is simple: shorten, share, then watch the clicks come in with the kind of detail you’d normally rent by the month.
Once a link is live, the analytics are the whole point. You see clicks as they land, where they came from, and what opened them — the difference between a cosmetic tool and one you’d actually use to decide something. Sensitive link? Put a password on it. Need it to be more than a redirect? The page hosting lets a short link be an actual page. None of it gated.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The closing argument
I’m not going to pretend a URL shortener will change your life. It’s plumbing. But plumbing should be free, fast, and free of surprise invoices, and most of it somehow isn’t. That’s the entire thesis: give people the part everyone else hides, ask nothing for it, and don’t start a clock the second the thing becomes useful.
If you shorten links often enough to be annoyed by paywalls, that’s who I built it for. Take it for a spin: https://sh0sh0.netlify.app
One more shortener, sure. This time the good part isn’t behind the curtain.
